answers.field_label: What Brings You To My Office Briefly Describe Your Current Situation
answers.value:
Over the past year, I’ve been struggling more and more with anxiety, low mood, and a sense of being completely overwhelmed by day-to-day life. I’ve always been a bit of a worrier, but recently it feels like it’s taken over. I’m having trouble sleeping, I’m irritable with my wife and kids, and I find it hard to concentrate at work. I work as a project manager for a software company, and things have been especially busy and chaotic since a reorganization last fall. I’m constantly worried about making mistakes, missing deadlines, or getting fired, even though my performance reviews have been fine.

At home, I feel mentally checked out a lot of the time. I catch myself zoning out on my phone to escape, and then I feel guilty for not being present with my kids. My wife has told me she feels like she’s “losing” me emotionally—that I’m there physically but not really engaged. I also notice that I’ve been avoiding social situations, even with close friends, because I feel drained and don’t want to have to pretend I’m okay. I’ve had several days where getting out of bed feels like a chore, even though I do it because I don’t want to let anyone down.

On top of that, I’ve been having physical symptoms—tightness in my chest, racing heart, and sometimes this feeling like I can’t take a full breath. I’ve gone to urgent care once thinking it might be something with my heart, but they said everything looked normal and suggested it might be anxiety. My appetite is up and down—some days I barely eat until dinner, other days I stress-eat junk food late at night. I’ve also noticed I’ve been drinking more beer in the evenings than I used to, not to the point of blacking out or anything, but enough that I’m concerned it’s becoming a crutch.

The main reason I’m seeking help now is that this is starting to affect my relationships and my sense of who I am. I feel like I’m failing as a dad and husband because I’m so preoccupied with work and my own thoughts. I used to enjoy hobbies like playing guitar, hiking, and coaching my son’s soccer team, but lately even those things feel like obligations rather than something I look forward to. I don’t feel suicidal, but I do sometimes have thoughts like, “If I just didn’t have to deal with any of this, it would be easier,” which scares me. I want to get ahead of this before it gets worse.

Overall, I’m looking for help understanding what’s going on with me—whether this is anxiety, depression, burnout, or some combination—and to learn healthier ways to cope so I can be more present, stable, and engaged in my life again.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: How Long Has This Been Occurring
answers.value:
In some ways, I think I’ve been prone to anxiety most of my life, but the symptoms have noticeably ramped up over the past 10–12 months. I can trace the more intense phase of this to around last April, when my company announced a major restructuring. Since then, the constant low-level worry has turned into more frequent episodes of panic-like symptoms and periods of feeling really down and disconnected. 

If I break it down:
- Mild anxiety and overthinking: pretty much as long as I can remember, but manageable until recently.
- Significant increase in stress and worry about work: about 10–12 months.
- Sleep problems (waking up in the middle of the night with racing thoughts): around 8 months.
- Feeling more withdrawn, losing interest in hobbies, and having more frequent “down” days: maybe the last 5–6 months.
- More noticeable impact on my marriage and parenting (snapping at my family, emotionally checked out): roughly the last 4 months.

The chest tightness and racing heart episodes started about 6 months ago and have been happening at least a few times a week, sometimes daily during more stressful periods.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: What Do You Believe Caused It
answers.value:
I think it’s a combination of work-related stress, unresolved past issues, and how I tend to handle (or not handle) my emotions. The most immediate trigger seems to be my job. I’ve been working as a project manager in a mid-size software company for about 7 years. Last year, the company went through a big reorganization—new leadership, layoffs in some departments, and shifting responsibilities. Even though I wasn’t laid off, I ended up with a heavier workload and less clarity about expectations. I now manage more projects at once, with tighter deadlines, and there’s this unspoken pressure to always be available and “on.”

I also tend to put a lot of pressure on myself. I grew up in a family where being responsible and not complaining were really emphasized. My dad worked long hours and rarely talked about his feelings, and my mom kept everything running at home without really asking for help. I internalized that I should just “push through” and not burden others with my problems. So when work got more stressful, instead of asking for support or setting boundaries, I just tried to keep saying yes and doing more. I think I’ve been running on adrenaline for a long time and my body is finally saying it’s too much.

I also suspect that becoming a parent of two young kids while trying to advance in my career has stretched me thinner than I realized. Our daughter is 8 and our son is 5, and between school, activities, and just the general chaos of family life, there’s not a lot of downtime. I love my kids, but I often feel like I’m failing both at work and at home because I can’t give 100% to either. That constant feeling of falling short feeds into the anxiety and low mood.

On a deeper level, I think I never really learned healthy ways to express or process emotions. When I feel overwhelmed, my default is to shut down or distract myself—scrolling on my phone, watching YouTube, or having a few beers to “take the edge off.” That might help in the moment, but it doesn’t solve anything and probably makes me feel worse long-term. I also tend to catastrophize—if something small goes wrong, my brain jumps straight to worst-case scenarios (e.g., “If this project is delayed, my boss will be furious, I’ll get a bad review, and then I’ll get fired and won’t be able to support my family”).

There have also been a couple of specific events in the last year that I think contributed. My dad had a minor heart attack about 9 months ago, which shook me up and made me more aware of my own health and mortality. Around the same time, one of my close friends moved out of state, and I didn’t realize how much I relied on him as someone I could casually vent to. Losing that regular, in-person support has made me feel more isolated.

So, in summary: chronic work stress, perfectionistic tendencies, difficulty setting boundaries, major life role strain (work + parenting), limited emotional coping skills, and a few stressful life events all seem to have piled up and led to where I am now.

answers.field_label: What Have You Tried To Change It
answers.value:
I’ve tried a handful of things, some more consistently than others, but nothing has really stuck or made a big enough difference so far.

1. **Self-help and lifestyle changes:**
   - **Exercise:** I started going for runs around my neighborhood a few months ago after reading that exercise can help with anxiety and mood. For a few weeks, I managed to run 2–3 times a week in the evenings. I did notice that on days I ran, I slept a little better and felt a bit less tense. But when work got especially busy, I let it slide and haven’t been consistent since. Now I might go for a run once every couple of weeks at best.
   - **Sleep hygiene:** I tried setting a rule for myself to be in bed by 11:00 p.m. and not look at my phone in bed. That worked for maybe two weeks, but then late-night work emails and my habit of scrolling to “wind down” crept back in. I also experimented with a white noise app and limiting caffeine after 2:00 p.m. Those helped somewhat, but again, I struggled to maintain the routine.
   - **Cutting back on alcohol:** I noticed I was drinking 2–3 beers most nights, so I tried to cut down to weekends only. I did okay with that for about a month, but slowly weeknights started creeping back in, especially after stressful days. I haven’t completely gone back to daily drinking, but I’m definitely not where I want to be.

2. **Work-related attempts:**
   - **Time management tools:** I’ve tried using different project management apps and to-do lists to feel more in control of my workload. I color-code tasks and set reminders, which sometimes helps me feel less scattered. However, the volume of work and the constant shifting priorities still make me feel overwhelmed, even with better organization.
   - **Setting boundaries (sort of):** I had one conversation with my manager about feeling stretched thin. He was understanding in the moment, and we adjusted one deadline, but I didn’t really push for any long-term changes. I tend to say “it’s fine, I’ll figure it out” even when it’s not fine. So, while I’ve tried to hint at needing support, I haven’t consistently advocated for myself.

3. **Social and emotional support:**
   - **Talking to my wife:** I’ve opened up to my wife more in the last couple of months, telling her that I feel anxious and down rather than just snapping or withdrawing. That has helped some—she’s been supportive and encourages me to take breaks or go for a walk when I’m overwhelmed. But I can tell she’s also tired and stressed from managing a lot at home, so I feel guilty leaning on her too much.
   - **Friends:** I’ve mentioned to a couple of close friends that I’ve been “really stressed” and “not myself,” but I usually keep it surface-level and joke it off. I haven’t allowed myself to be fully honest about how bad it’s felt at times.

4. **Information and self-education:**
   - I’ve read articles and watched videos about anxiety, burnout, and depression. I even downloaded a couple of mental health apps for guided meditations and breathing exercises. I did some of the breathing exercises during particularly anxious moments, and they helped a little with the immediate physical symptoms. The problem is that I don’t stick with them regularly; I tend to only use them when I’m already in crisis mode.

5. **Medical check-up:**
   - After one really bad episode of chest tightness and racing heart about 6 months ago, I went to urgent care. They ran some basic tests and told me everything looked normal and suggested stress and anxiety as likely causes. They recommended following up with my primary care doctor. I did have a follow-up appointment where my doctor talked to me briefly about stress management and gave me a list of local therapists and online resources. I held onto that list for a while and only recently started actually reaching out, which is what led me here.

Overall, while I’ve made some attempts to address this on my own, they’ve been inconsistent and mostly focused on surface-level changes. I haven’t had a structured, ongoing plan or professional support. That’s why I’m seeking therapy now—I feel like I need guidance, accountability, and tools that go beyond what I’ve been able to do on my own.

answers.field_label: Who Lives In Your Home With You
answers.value:
I live in a three-bedroom townhouse with my wife and our two children.

- **Emily** – my wife, age 36. We’ve been married for 10 years. She works part-time as a nurse at a local clinic and also handles a lot of the household logistics—school communication, kids’ schedules, etc. She’s generally supportive and caring, but we’ve both been stressed lately and sometimes end up snapping at each other over small things.
- **Sophie** – our daughter, age 8. She’s in third grade at the local elementary school. She’s energetic, talkative, and a bit sensitive. She does dance class twice a week and loves reading and drawing. Recently she’s been asking why I’m on my phone so much and has commented that I seem “grumpy” more often, which hurts to hear.
- **Lucas** – our son, age 5. He’s in kindergarten. He’s very attached to both me and my wife and can be a bit of a handful when he’s tired. He loves building with Legos and playing soccer in the backyard. He doesn’t fully understand what’s going on, of course, but he definitely picks up on tension in the house.

We don’t have any other relatives living with us. My parents live about 45 minutes away in Littleton, and we see them at least once or twice a month. My wife’s parents live out of state (in Minnesota), so we see them a few times a year, usually around holidays or during summer break. Day to day, it’s just the four of us in the home.

answers.field_label: How Would You Describe Your Current Relationship
answers.value:
Overall, I would describe my relationship with Emily as loving but currently strained and under a lot of pressure. We’ve been together for 12 years (10 married), and we have a solid foundation of shared values—family, honesty, and trying to be good partners and parents. We still say “I love you” regularly, we hug, and we do have moments of genuine connection, like laughing together over something the kids did or watching a show together after they go to bed.

That said, the last year has definitely been harder on us. A lot of our conversations have shifted from being about our interests or feelings to being strictly logistical: who’s picking up which kid, what bills are due, what’s for dinner, etc. When I’m overwhelmed with work, I tend to retreat into myself—either getting lost in my phone or zoning out in front of the TV. Emily experiences that as me being distant and uninterested, and she has told me she feels like she’s carrying the emotional load for the whole family.

We’ve had more arguments lately, often about seemingly small things that are really about bigger underlying issues. For example, she’ll get upset that I forgot to sign a school form or that I’m late getting home, and I’ll get defensive because I already feel like I’m failing. This can escalate into her saying she feels like she’s parenting a third child sometimes, and me shutting down or saying something curt that I regret later. We usually apologize afterward, but it feels like we’re stuck in a loop.

Intimacy has also been affected—not just sexually, but emotionally. We still sleep in the same bed and are affectionate, but there’s less spontaneity and playfulness. When I’m anxious or down, I don’t feel very connected to my own emotions, which makes it hard to connect deeply with her. She’s encouraged me to seek therapy and has said she wants to support me through this, which I appreciate. At the same time, I know my current state is putting a strain on her and on our relationship.

In short: we love each other and are committed to staying together and working things out, but we’re both tired, stressed, and not always communicating in the healthiest way. I’m hoping that by getting help for myself, it will also improve our relationship and help us feel more like a team again.

persona.education: B.S. in Information Systems, University of Colorado Denver (2009); Scrum Master certification (CSM, 2016); PMP certification (2021)
persona.background_context:
This is Michael Aaron Delgado, a 38-year-old senior project manager living in a three-bedroom townhouse in southeast Aurora, Colorado, near the Cherry Creek State Park area. He shares the home with his wife Emily, 36, and their two kids, Sophie, 8, and Lucas, 5. They’ve been in this townhouse for about five years, having moved from a smaller apartment in central Aurora when Lucas was born and they needed more space and a small yard. The neighborhood is a mix of young families and older couples; there’s a community playground a short walk away where the kids ride their scooters when the weather is decent.

Michael works for FrontRange Logic, Inc., a mid-size software company based in Denver that builds custom web applications and internal tools for regional businesses—healthcare groups, logistics companies, and some local government contracts. He’s been with the company for seven years and was promoted to senior project manager about two years ago. Since a major reorganization last April, his role has become more chaotic: he now juggles four to six active projects at a time, supervising two offshore developers and coordinating with designers, QA testers, and demanding clients. His days are packed with back-to-back Zoom calls, Jira board grooming, and status reports. He typically works from home three days a week and commutes into the downtown Denver office on Tuesdays and Thursdays, a 35–40 minute drive if traffic is light, closer to an hour when it’s snowing or during rush hour on I-225.

On a typical weekday, Michael’s alarm goes off around 6:15 a.m. He usually hits snooze once or twice because he’s had another restless night—waking up at 3:00 a.m. with his mind racing about an upcoming deployment or an email he forgot to send. By 6:45 he’s in the kitchen making coffee and pouring cereal for the kids while Emily packs Sophie’s and Lucas’s lunches. Mornings can be tense; if Sophie can’t find a homework sheet or Lucas refuses to put on shoes, Michael feels his patience fray faster than it used to. He often catches himself snapping, then apologizing on the drive to school. After drop-off at the nearby elementary school, he either heads back to his home office—a small converted guest room with a desk pressed against the window—or starts the commute downtown.

His workday is a blur of Slack pings, Teams meetings, and shifting priorities. Since the reorg, one of his biggest stressors is the implicit expectation that he be available at all hours. His manager, Jason, is based in another time zone and tends to send messages early in the morning and late at night. Michael feels pressure to respond quickly, even when he’s technically off the clock. He’s good at his job and his performance reviews reflect that, but he struggles to internalize the positive feedback; instead, he fixates on small oversights, worrying they’re signs he’s about to drop the ball in a big way. A few months ago, a client escalated a complaint about a delayed feature, and even though the delay was due to a vendor issue outside his control, he still thinks about it whenever he feels his chest tighten.

Evenings are a mix of family time and exhaustion. On nights when Emily works her part-time shifts at a nearby clinic, Michael handles pick-up, dinner, and bedtime mostly on his own. He can manage the logistics—throwing together pasta, supervising homework, refereeing arguments over the tablet—but emotionally he often feels distant, like he’s going through the motions. When Emily is home, they try to sit down for dinner together around 6:30 p.m., asking the kids about their day. Sophie talks excitedly about dance class or a book she’s reading, and Lucas demands someone come outside and kick the soccer ball with him. Michael wants to be fully present in those moments but often catches his mind drifting back to a tricky stakeholder or an upcoming sprint review.

After the kids are in bed, usually by 8:30 or 9:00 p.m., Michael and Emily clean up the kitchen and then either sit on the couch to watch a show or retreat into their separate screens. Over the last year, the latter has become more common. Michael will tell himself he’s just going to check email quickly or scroll Reddit for a few minutes, and suddenly it’s 11:45 p.m. and he’s on his third beer, watching YouTube videos about guitar gear or productivity hacks he never quite implements. He knows the late-night screen time and drinking make his sleep worse, but when he’s keyed up from the day, not doing anything feels harder than numbing out.

The family is close to his parents, who live in Littleton, about a 45-minute drive away. His dad, Robert, recently retired from a long career in commercial construction and had a minor heart attack nine months ago. That event rattled Michael; he spent the night at the hospital with his mom, Karen, listening to the beeping monitors and thinking about his own stress levels and whether he was heading down the same path. His dad recovered well and is now on medication and a cardiac rehab program, but the scare lingered in Michael’s mind and intensified his health-related anxiety, especially when his own chest tightness and racing heart episodes began.

Michael’s younger brother Daniel lives in Fort Collins and works as a high school science teacher. They talk every few weeks and text memes back and forth, but they don’t see each other as often as they’d like. Emily’s parents, Pat and Mark, are in Minnesota and visit two or three times a year, usually for a week in the summer and at either Thanksgiving or Christmas. When they’re in town, Emily seems visibly lighter because she has more help with the kids, and Michael feels both relieved and guilty—relieved for the support, guilty that it takes out-of-state grandparents visiting to make things feel manageable.

Before this recent stretch of heightened anxiety and low mood, Michael had a fairly balanced set of hobbies. He’s been playing guitar since high school and used to meet a couple of friends once a month for informal jam sessions in a friend’s basement in Parker. He also enjoyed hiking local trails on weekend mornings—places like Deer Creek Canyon or sections of the Colorado Trail—often taking Sophie along for easier hikes when she was younger. He volunteered as an assistant coach for Lucas’s Saturday morning soccer team last fall, but this season he stepped back to “just helping when I can,” telling himself it was because of work. In reality, even the idea of committing to a regular weekend activity felt overwhelming.

The past 10–12 months have been marked by a gradual but noticeable shift in his internal landscape. What started as manageable worry about deadlines grew into persistent tension and episodes that feel close to panic. The chest tightness and racing heart, which began about six months ago, have become a regular part of his week. After the urgent care visit that ruled out an immediate physical problem, he felt both relieved and frustrated—relieved that it wasn’t a heart attack, frustrated that the explanation of “stress and anxiety” didn’t come with an easy fix. He followed up with his primary care doctor, who suggested stress management strategies and gave him a list of therapists and resources. That list sat folded in his desk drawer for months before he finally started acting on it.

Emotionally, Michael feels caught between roles and expectations. He wants to be a dependable provider, an engaged father, and a supportive partner, yet he often feels like he’s failing at all three. When he forgets to sign a permission slip or is late to Sophie’s school performance because a meeting ran over, he berates himself internally. When Emily sighs and says she feels like she’s doing the emotional heavy lifting for the family, he oscillates between defensive frustration and shame. Their arguments are rarely explosive, but they’ve become more frequent, often triggered by small things that tap into bigger fears—her feeling like she’s raising a third child, his fear that he’s letting everyone down.

Despite the strain, there are still pockets of warmth and connection that keep him grounded. Sunday mornings, if they’re not rushing to an activity, they make pancakes together—Sophie mixes the batter, Lucas cracks eggs with help, and Michael flips pancakes while Emily makes coffee. Occasionally, he’ll catch a glimpse of the life he wants to feel fully present in: laughing at Lucas’s messy face, listening to Sophie explain her latest drawing, and exchanging a tired but genuine smile with Emily across the table. Those moments remind him why he’s motivated to understand what’s happening inside him and to find healthier ways to cope.

Recently, he’s been trying to reintroduce small, manageable changes. In January he started taking short walks around the block during the workday instead of scrolling on his phone between meetings. On some evenings, he’ll pick up his acoustic guitar for 10 minutes after the kids go to bed, even if he doesn’t feel like it, just to reconnect with something that used to bring him joy. He set a loose personal goal to limit beer to two or three nights a week instead of most nights, with mixed success. He’s also begun to be more honest with Emily about what he’s feeling, saying “I’m really anxious today” instead of just withdrawing. Those efforts are imperfect and inconsistent, but they mark a shift from trying to push everything down to cautiously acknowledging that he needs help and that change, even if slow and incremental, is possible.

As he moves through his daily life—commuting along the Front Range, sitting in stand-ups with his development teams, helping Sophie with math homework, or reading Lucas a bedtime story—Michael is very much in the middle of his story, not at the end of it. He’s wrestling with questions about identity, responsibility, and what it means to be present in his own life. He’s aware that his anxiety, low mood, and coping habits have begun to shape not just his internal experience but also his relationships. Underneath the worry and exhaustion is a strong desire to feel more stable, to reclaim enjoyment in his hobbies and family time, and to build a life that doesn’t feel like a constant tightrope walk between burnout and obligation.

question_secrets.question_text: What Brings You To My Office Briefly Describe Your Current Situation

secrets.detail_content: What finally pushed me to reach out was a panic episode I had on January 18, 2025, during a status meeting with my director, Melissa Grant, at the FrontRange Logic office in downtown Denver. My chest got so tight I thought I was having a heart attack, and they ended up calling an ambulance to take me to Denver Health Medical Center. The ER doctor there, Dr. Ravi Patel, told me my heart was fine and strongly recommended I see a therapist because it was likely a severe anxiety attack. Since then, I’ve been terrified it will happen again at work and that Melissa and HR will see me as unstable or unreliable.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses identifiable medical treatment details, specific providers, employer and supervisor names, and a potentially stigmatizing workplace medical incident that could affect employment or be seen as over-sharing for a brief intake question. It goes beyond describing symptoms and motivation for therapy into detailed, traceable medical and employment information.

events: ER evaluation for suspected heart attack

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Follow-up from incident on 1/18

Hi Melissa,
I wanted to thank you again for staying calm during the episode I had in yesterday’s status meeting and for calling 911. The ER at Denver Health ruled out any cardiac issues and said it was a severe panic attack. I’m going to follow up with a therapist as they recommended.
Personal note (1/20/25): Panic attack in Melissa’s meeting at FrontRange, ended up in Denver Health ER. Dr. Patel said heart is fine, likely anxiety. Need to get help before this happens again.

secrets.detail_content: A big part of why I’m here is how my anxiety is affecting my parenting. In late November 2024, after a 60-hour week trying to hit a release deadline at the FrontRange Logic Tech Center office, I completely lost my temper and yelled at my daughter Sophie for spilling juice on my work laptop at our house in Aurora. I slammed my hand on the kitchen table so hard that it scared both Sophie and my son Lucas, and Sophie later told my wife Emily that she was afraid to ask me for help with her math homework now. Since that night, I’ve noticed I avoid being alone with the kids in the evenings because I’m worried I’ll snap again.
secrets.why_sensitive: This includes specific, potentially incriminating details about anger outbursts toward minor children, with names, ages, and locations, which could raise mandated-reporting concerns or bias the clinician beyond what’s necessary to understand the presenting problem. It goes beyond a general description of irritability into a detailed incident that might have legal or child-welfare implications.

appears_naturally_in.example: I totally lost it on Sophie last night over a stupid juice spill on my work laptop after another 60-hour week. Slammed my hand on the table and scared both kids. I feel like the worst dad. I think I need to talk to someone about my stress and anger.

Subject: About last night

Emily,
I’ve been thinking about how I yelled at Sophie in the kitchen. Working crazy hours at the Tech Center office is getting to me, but that’s no excuse. Seeing how scared she was makes me realize I need help managing this anxiety and irritability.

secrets.detail_content: My anxiety really escalated after an informal performance discussion with my VP, Jason Miller, in early October 2024 in his office at FrontRange Logic. He told me there had been ‘concerns’ about my responsiveness and that if I couldn’t handle multiple concurrent projects, they might need to ‘revisit my role’ in Q1 2025. Since that conversation, I’ve been obsessively checking email until 1–2 a.m. most nights, and I wake up at 5:30 a.m. to get a jump on Slack messages. I’m constantly worried I’m going to be put on a performance improvement plan or laid off, which is making it hard to focus on anything else.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals detailed, identifiable employment information, including a named executive, implied performance risk, and potential job insecurity that could have serious professional and financial consequences if shared beyond a strictly confidential setting. It is more specific than needed to convey work-related stress as a reason for seeking therapy.

I’m not blacking out, but I’ve noticed I’m using beer to numb out my anxiety and dread about work the next day.

question_secrets.question_text: How Long Has This Been Occurring

Anyway, didn’t mean to unload this much, but you’re one of the few people I can be straight with. How are things up in Fort Collins? Still surviving high schoolers?

I tried to push back last October with Kevin and HR (Lisa) and even brought a printout of the UCHealth cardiologist’s note from 9/29/23 saying my chest pain was stress‑related. Kevin shifted one tiny account; Lisa mostly asked if I was still “fit for duty,” which just made me more paranoid.

The anxiety hit a new level on April 9, 2024, when I had what I’m pretty sure was a panic attack in the Maple Ridge Elementary parking lot dropping Sophie for her 2nd‑grade field trip. Sat in the car 20 minutes feeling like I couldn’t breathe, then rolled into a 9 a.m. Alpine Data Systems status call 30 minutes late. Jason called later asking why I sounded “off.” Since then, any time I’m near the school or that 7th‑floor conference room downtown, my chest tightens.

Home is its own circus. Emily’s mom, Pat, was here part of May after a huge fight with Mark about his gambling debt, and before that my cousin Elena and her little guy Mateo crashed on our couch for a few months when she left an abusive relationship and was working with SafeHouse Denver. We love them, but the townhouse feels smaller every year.

On the plus side, Lucas’s Saturday soccer is back, and I’m “assistant assistant coach,” which mostly means handing out orange slices. Tiny humans yelling “GOOOOAL” is surprisingly grounding.

You still up for that guys’ weekend hike this summer, or are we all officially old now?

Mike

Personal journal – 2025-01-20

I keep thinking about how this all snowballed.

Timeline check:

– Nov 3, 2023: Dad’s minor heart attack at St. Anthony in Lakewood. Sat in the ER with Mom while Dr. Patel explained the blocked artery and stent. That’s when I started checking my own blood pressure every night at home and Googling symptoms like a maniac.

– Late June 2024: at Dad’s house in Lakewood, he tells me the prostate cancer is back and they’re talking about radiation at St. Joseph. Since then I’ve been waking up at 2–3 a.m. almost every night, mind racing.

– April 9, 2024: panic in the Maple Ridge Elementary parking lot before Sophie’s field trip. 20 minutes sitting in the car, then 30 minutes late to the 9 a.m. Alpine Data Systems status call.

– August 22, 2024: Jason’s written warning in the 9th‑floor conference room about the Denver Health mobile app deliverable and “re‑evaluating my role” if billable hours and “client‑facing demeanor” don’t improve.

– Late November 2024: 60‑hour week at the Tech Center office, came home fried, and lost it on Sophie for spilling juice on my work laptop. Slammed my hand on the kitchen table, scared both kids.

– January 18, 2025: panic episode in Melissa’s status meeting at the downtown FrontRange office, ambulance to Denver Health, Dr. Ravi Patel in the ER saying heart is fine, severe anxiety attack.

Layer on top of that 3–4 IPAs most weeknights since around Labor Day 2024, sometimes 6 on Fridays after the kids go down, and Emily confronting me Dec 2 when she saw the $186 at Mile High Liquors. No wonder I feel like I’m coming apart.

Goal: small changes. Walks. Guitar 10 minutes. Two dry nights a week, non‑negotiable.

Subject: Quick update on Dad and the chaos here

Hi Mom,

Just wanted to send a proper update instead of bits and pieces over text.

I’m glad Dad’s doing better since the stent at St. Anthony back in November 2023. That night in the ER with Dr. Patel explaining the blocked artery scared me more than I admitted. When he told us they caught it in time, I remember thinking, “He’s 65, I’m 38, and I’m already having chest pain too.” Then last summer at the Lakewood house when Dad told me the prostate cancer had come back and they were talking about radiation at St. Joseph, my sleep really went off the rails. I’ve been waking up around 2–3 a.m. most nights since then.

On top of that, work has been nonstop. Jason gave me that written warning in August about the Denver Health mobile app project, and then in early October he pulled me into his office to say there were “concerns” about my responsiveness and they might revisit my role in Q1 2025. That’s when the obsessive late‑night email checking started.

Home has been full too. Elena and Mateo staying with us January–March 2023 while she worked with SafeHouse Denver was the right thing, but it was a lot in a small townhouse. Then last May, Pat stayed May 1–21 after the big fight with Mark over his gambling debt. Again, good to help, but it felt like we never had our own space.

The kids are okay—Sophie’s into graphic novels, Lucas is still soccer‑obsessed. We’re trying to keep Sunday pancakes as a ritual no matter what.

How are you holding up with all of Dad’s appointments?

Love,
Michael

Therapy prep – things I’ve already tried / messed with

1. Meds

– March 2024: did an online psychiatry intake with MindWay Clinic from a conference room at the FrontRange Tech Center. NP Sarah Klein started me on 25 mg sertraline. Stayed on it ~3 weeks, then stopped on 3/28/24 because I freaked out about it being in my record when MetLife did the life insurance screening. Never went to the 4/12 follow‑up.

– Late August 2023: during that brutal insomnia week, I took one of Mom’s leftover 0.5 mg lorazepam from her surgery at St. Anthony—around midnight, without telling Emily. Slept, but felt guilty and scared afterward. Didn’t repeat it.

2. Therapy-ish stuff

– January 2023: signed up for a 6‑week Tuesday night (7–8 p.m.) online CBT group through Denver Behavioral Wellness. Made it to two sessions. Bailed after a breakout where a teacher named Rachel described panic attacks in front of her class—too close to my near‑panic during the December 2022 sprint review at FrontRange.

– May 15, 2022: couples session with Pastor Jim at GracePoint Community Church. He had me keep a “sin journal” for three weeks—emailing him every time I got angry, drank more than 3 beers, or felt resentful (including about Pat and Mark after that tense Memorial Day BBQ). Stopped because it felt like I was just cataloging failures.

3. Work boundaries

– October 2023: met with Kevin and Lisa at the downtown office, brought the 9/29/23 UCHealth cardiology letter saying chest pain is stress‑related. Got one small client reassigned; left feeling like HR saw me as a liability.

Need to remember: I’ve tried things. The problem is sticking with them, not that nothing works.

Subject: Things have been rough with Em (need to vent)

Hey Javi,

I don’t really talk about this stuff with many people, but you’ve known me since the dorm days, so here goes.

Emily and I are in a weird place. She’s still upset about a lot of things that have piled up: the money stuff, my drinking, and how checked‑out I’ve been with the kids. Back in March 2023 I quietly took out a $12,000 personal loan at the Wells Fargo branch downtown to cover some credit card debt and NBA betting losses. She found the statements on 11/10/2024 and we spent that whole weekend at the house arguing about whether she could trust me with money. She threatened separate accounts if I ever hide anything like that again.

Layer on top of that her mom Pat staying with us last May 1–21 after the blow‑up with Mark over his gambling debt, plus Elena and Mateo on our couch January–March 2023 after she left her abusive ex and started working with SafeHouse Denver. It feels like our three‑bedroom Aurora townhouse has been a revolving door of crises.

Meanwhile, I’m working 50–60 hours a week, drinking 3–4 IPAs most nights since the FrontRange reorg around March 2024, sometimes 6 on Fridays. Emily confronted me on December 2 after she saw I’d spent $186 at Mile High Liquors in a month. She’s also still shaken from that night in late November when I slammed my hand on the kitchen table and yelled at Sophie over the juice spill on my work laptop after a 60‑hour week at the Tech Center office. She told me later she felt like she was “losing” me.

I love her and the kids, but right now our relationship feels like it’s held together by logistics and Sunday pancakes.

Thanks for listening, man.

Mike
